did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper explicitly uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), a deep learning method, to classify dermoscopic images of skin lesions (including melanoma, keratosis, and basal cell carcinoma) from the HAM10000 dataset. The study reports results such as 97.92% recognition rate and 87.6% test set accuracy using CNN models.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) on the HAM10000 dataset to classify skin lesions. It includes specific sections for Methodology (data acquisition, augmentation, model training) and Results (reporting accuracy metrics like 87.6% test set accuracy and loss values). Although published in a conference proceedings (ICAIT-2024), it reports new empirical results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Dermoscopic Impression Using ABCD Rule, the 7-point Checklist and The Menzies Scoring Method
journal: 2024 Second International Conference on Advances in Information Technology (ICAIT-2024)
brief_summary: This study investigates the use of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for classifying dermoscopic images of skin lesions, including melanoma, keratosis, and basal cell carcinoma, using the HAM10000 dataset. The methodology involved data acquisition of 10,015 skin lesions, resizing images to 220x220 pixels, and applying data augmentation techniques such as horizontal flipping to address class imbalance. The model was evaluated using metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score across 10 training epochs. The CNN achieved a test set accuracy of 87.6% and a recognition rate of 97.92%, demonstrating its potential to assist dermatologists in early skin cancer detection. The authors conclude that while CNNs perform well, further research with larger and more diverse datasets is needed before clinical implementation.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied as a primary malignant skin cancer; mentioned in the context of classification, histological thickness analysis (Table 2), and comparison with benign lesions.
Included in the HAM10000 dataset; the paper notes that 'Star spots and keratosis appear in the same way as cancer' and discusses binary classification of keratosis.
Included in the HAM10000 dataset; mentioned in the context of binary classification experiments against keratosis.
Mentioned in the context of binary classification experiments (keratosis vs. basal cell carcinoma and epithelial cell carcinoma).
Referenced in the conclusion regarding the study's survey of deep nervous systems against 'benign and dangerous tumours' and in references to melanoma/nevus discrimination.
Mentioned in the conclusion regarding binary classification of freckles versus star-shaped freckles.
Used as a broad category for the dataset (10,015 skin lesions) and the overall diagnostic goal.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (e.g., melanoma, keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, nevus, freckle) using the HAM10000 dataset.
all_task_types.details: The paper uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to classify dermoscopic images into specific disease classes. The study reports classification metrics including accuracy (87.6% on test set, 97.92% recognition rate), precision, recall, and F1-score. It explicitly mentions binary classification tasks such as 'melanoma vs skin cancer', 'keratosis vs basal cell carcinoma', and 'freckles versus star-shaped freckles'.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine with 10000 training images)
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset containing 10,015 skin lesions collected from different sources. The dataset includes metadata such as patient age, gender, image ID, diagnosis type, and geographic location. Images are stored in JPEG format with a resolution of 600x450 pixels.

use.training: Used for training the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model. The dataset was subjected to data augmentation (horizontal flip) and resizing (to 220x220 pixels, then scaled to 96x96 for training). The study mentions balancing the classes, resulting in 200 images per group after augmentation.
use.validation: Used for validation during the 10 training epochs to monitor loss and accuracy.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the final model performance, achieving a test set accuracy of 87.6%.
use.other: Used for binary classification experiments (e.g., melanoma vs. skin cancer, keratosis vs. basal cell carcinoma, freckles vs. star-shaped freckles).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To address class imbalance in the dataset by extending the interaction until the distance of the larger class is reached.

statistical_methods_used.method: Loss function analysis (Learning loss, Validation loss, Verification loss, Proof loss, Evidence loss)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To monitor model performance and convergence during the 10 training epochs.
To evaluate the performance and reliability of the CNN-based model for classifying and analyzing image data.
To report the final performance of the model on unseen data.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported descriptive performance metrics such as accuracy (87.6% on test set, 97.92% recognition rate), precision, recall, and F1-score, along with training/validation loss values over 10 epochs.

HAM10000.notes: The paper reports an 'Actual exam' value of 0.875757576118816. It states that CNN performed well in binary classification of freckles versus star-shaped freckles, and slightly better in the binary classification of keratosis and basal cell carcinoma and epithelial cell carcinoma. The study notes that star spots and keratosis appear in the same way as cancer. No comparison with skin cancer achieved higher accuracy for AN than for basal and epithelial cell-diagnosed keratosis.

architecture_used.input_size: 220x220 pixels (resized from original 600x450), scaled to 96x96 for training

architecture_details.description: The paper describes CNNs as computer models inspired by the human body, proven effective for image classification. Specific architectural layers (e.g., number of convolutions, pooling types, dense layers) are not detailed.

training_details.class_imbalance_handling: Random oversampling to balance classes (200 images per group)

preprocessing_techniques: Image scaling to 96x96 pixels for training
Random oversampling to address class imbalance (balancing classes to 200 images per group)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to image pixels horizontally to generate synthetic data and address class imbalance. Used during CNN model training to help the model learn differently than without augmentation.
Used to address class imbalance by extending the interaction until the distance of the larger class is reached. This resulted in a balanced dataset of 200 images per group (totaling 200*7 images).

conclusions: The proposed Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model achieved a recognition rate of 97.92% and a test set accuracy of 87.6% in classifying skin lesions.
The study demonstrates that CNNs have the potential to assist dermatologists in the early detection of skin diseases, including melanoma, keratosis, and basal cell carcinoma.
The CNN model performed well in the binary classification of freckles versus star-shaped freckles.
The CNN model performed slightly better in the binary classification of keratosis versus basal cell carcinoma and epithelial cell carcinoma.
The study acknowledges limitations regarding the lack of statistical accuracy in results, noting that significant differences in AUCs often go undetected.
The authors conclude that further research with larger, more diverse datasets (including different groups and ages) and more dermoscopy images is needed before clinical implementation.
The authors suggest that additional comparative studies with dermatologists are necessary to validate the use of CNNs in real clinical settings.
The study highlights the importance of using image metadata to determine the accuracy of the model.
important_remarks: The paper acknowledges a significant limitation: 'The lack of statistical accuracy in the results also weakens the results because significant differences in AUCs often go undetected.'
The study explicitly states that further research with 'larger, more diverse datasets (including different groups and ages)' and 'more dermoscopy images' is needed before clinical implementation.
The authors note that 'simultaneous updating of the 6 tests are needed to inspire change,' suggesting the current evaluation metrics or tests may be insufficient.

The paper highlights ethical and deployment concerns, noting that 'important considerations regarding patient privacy, informed consent, transparency of data ownership, and data security' must be addressed for smartphone-based CAD systems.
The model was trained on a heavily balanced dataset (200 images per group) created via random oversampling, which may not reflect real-world class distributions.
The authors admit that 'science cannot follow all aspects of philosophy' regarding the classification of melanoma, indicating potential ambiguity in ground truth or diagnostic criteria.

The study recommends 'additional comparative studies that need to be tested before using CNNs in a real clinical setting,' indicating a lack of external validation against dermatologists in this work.

The paper notes that 'star spots and keratosis appear in the same way as cancer,' highlighting a specific diagnostic challenge where benign lesions mimic malignant ones, which the model must overcome.
